claims.claim: Safari 26 was the first browser to ship native CSS masonry layout support, while Chrome and Firefox support requires experimental flags as of early 2026 and stable support is expected later in 2026.
claims.quote: Safari 26 just shipped it first... Chrome and Firefox support behind experimental flags, expected to ship stable support later in 2026

claims.claim: The CSS masonry layout is triggered via grid-template-rows: masonry, using standard grid syntax rather than a new display mode.
claims.quote: Primary syntax: grid-template-rows: masonry; triggers masonry packing on the row axis

claims.claim: CSS Grid Lanes masonry requires no JavaScript for packing logic — the browser handles all item placement natively.
claims.quote: The browser handles all the packing logic natively (no JavaScript required)

claims.claim: CSS Grid Lanes preserves DOM order for accessibility and keyboard navigation, unlike the CSS multi-column hack where visual order differs from DOM order.
claims.quote: CSS Grid Lanes preserves DOM order, contrasting with the column hack where items are ordered vertically...rather than naturally; the column method breaks accessibility because the DOM order doesn't match visual order

claims.claim: The recommended progressive enhancement fallback for browsers that do not support masonry is a regular grid with align-items: start, gated behind a @supports (grid-template-rows: masonry) feature query.
claims.quote: Recommended fallback: regular grid with align-items: start for unsupported browsers. Implementation via @supports (grid-template-rows: masonry) feature queries
